skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit AI images through the inference.sh CLI using FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, Topaz, and related models.
skill.summary: Generate, edit, stitch, and upscale AI images with inference.sh image models from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: tool-belt

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and URL patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are mainly documented inference.sh CLI examples and public documentation links, but the skill still enables external command execution and third-party image generation requests.

medium_findings.title: External Inference CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash access for infsh and provides many infsh app run examples. This is expected for the skill purpose, but prompts, image URLs, and outputs are handled by an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly allows Bash(infsh *) and documents infsh commands that call remote image apps. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is data exposure and command scope rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Image URL Processing
medium_findings.description: The upscaling and stitching examples accept remote image URLs. Users could expose private image links or controlled URLs to the inference service if they follow these examples without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show image_url and images fields containing external URLs. Legitimate image workflows often need URLs, but this still creates a clear privacy and data-sharing risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Public Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged public links to inference.sh pages, a hosted preview image, and install documentation. These are expected documentation references and do not show secret exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or image links and no credentials or local data are appended to them. They are still noted because users should understand the skill depends on a third-party service.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography finding points to the description line. No cryptographic operation, hash function call, encryption routine, or credential handling is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter describing image models and triggers. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms or cryptographic code in that location.

low_findings.title: External Skill Installation Examples
low_findings.description: The related-skills section suggests npx skills add commands for additional community skills. This is user-directed supply-chain exposure, not automatic installation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete external installation examples. They are not executed by the skill automatically, so the risk depends on whether users choose to run them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission for infsh
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands. This is necessary for the CLI workflow but should be treated as external command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration directly permits infsh execution. The command scope is limited to infsh, which lowers but does not remove operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Service and URL Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references inference.sh and examples with remote image URLs. These patterns can transmit prompts and image references outside the local workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation and examples clearly depend on a hosted inference platform. The pattern is legitimate, but users need notice before sending private prompts or images.

content.value_statement: Image teams need fast access to many generation models without switching tools. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh image workflows.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh image apps from supported AI coding tools.
Provides examples for text-to-image generation with FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, and Reve models.
Shows image editing, upscaling, and image stitching workflows through infsh app commands.
Lists model choices with practical use notes for quality, speed, text rendering, and cost.
Links users to inference.sh documentation for app running and image generation details.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI to be installed and authenticated before use.
Sends prompts, image URLs, and job data to a third-party inference service.
Does not include local image generation models or offline execution.
Model availability, pricing, and output quality depend on inference.sh and provider changes.

use_cases.title: Create product mockups
use_cases.description: Generate polished product scenes and campaign visuals from short prompts inside an AI assistant workflow.

use_cases.title: Explore concept art
use_cases.description: Compare multiple image models for characters, environments, posters, and visual directions.

use_cases.title: Automate image variants
use_cases.description: Run repeatable CLI image jobs for thumbnails, social assets, stitched images, and upscaled outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to generate a square image of a modern desk setup. Choose a balanced model and keep the prompt concise.

prompt_templates.title: Create branded social artwork
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three social media image concepts for a new productivity app. Use a 16:9 aspect ratio and include short prompt notes for each result.

prompt_templates.title: Upscale an approved image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the upscaling workflow for this image URL after confirming it is safe to share with inference.sh. Preserve the original composition.

prompt_templates.title: Compare model options
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend two inference.sh image models for cinematic product photography, then run the best option with a detailed prompt and practical settings.

output_examples.input: Generate a product photo of a ceramic coffee mug with studio lighting.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a suitable image model, runs the inference.sh command, and returns the generated image result with the model used.

output_examples.input: Make a poster image with clear text that says HELLO WORLD.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses a model known for text rendering and reports the generated poster output with a concise prompt summary.

output_examples.input: Upscale this public image URL for a presentation slide.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the URL can be shared, runs the upscaling app, and returns the enhanced image result.

best_practices: Confirm that prompts and image URLs are safe to send to inference.sh before running commands.
Start with a small prompt and one model, then iterate with clearer visual details.
Use model notes to choose between speed, cost, text rendering, editing, and upscaling quality.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential designs, private image URLs, or personal data without approval.
Do not install related skills from external sources without reviewing their contents first.
Do not assume every listed model supports the same settings, aspect ratios, or editing features.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to run hosted image generation and editing apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Do I need an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. The quick start requires infsh login before running image generation commands.

faq.question: Can it edit or upscale existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for image editing, image stitching, and Topaz upscaling apps.

faq.question: What data leaves my workspace?
faq.answer: Prompts, command inputs, image URLs, and job data may be sent to inference.sh or the selected provider.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning. Users should understand the external CLI, network, and data-sharing risks.
